title: 6 3 Quadric Surfaces
body:
6.3 QUADRIC SURFACES


When a standard quadric surface

                                       x2 y 2 z 2
                                      ± 2 ± 2 ± 2 =1
                                       a   b   c
is rotated in space mixed terms xy, xz and yz appear in the equation. It is possible to
express the resulting quadratic form in matrix form xT Ax = 1 where A is a symmet-
ric matrix. An analysis of the eigenvectors and eigenvalues of A will reveal both the
geometry and the principal axes of the surface.
         x2 y 2
    •       + 2 = 1 is an ellipse in R2 . (++)
         a2  b

         x2 y 2
    •       − 2 = 1 is a hyperbola in R2 (+−).
         a2  b

         x2 y 2 z 2
    •      2
             + 2 + 2 = 1 is an ellipsoid in R3 (+ + +).
         a    b   c

         x2 y 2 z 2
    •       + 2 − 2 = 1 is a hyperboloid of one sheet in R3 (+ + −).
         a2  b   c
         x2 y 2 z 2
            − 2 − 2 = 1 is a hyperboloid of 2 sheets in R3 (+ − −).
         a2  b   c


Example 1 Sketch each of the following curves. Find the smallest distance from the
curve to the origin in each case and determine the point(s) on the curve where this minimal
distance is achieved.
                          x2 y 2                           x2 y 2
                     a)      +   =1                   b)      −   = 1.
                          16   9                           25   4




        F a) Shortest distance of 3 at (0, ±3) b) Shortest distance of 5 at (±5, 0) F

                                             1
 Example 2 Sketch each of the following quadric surfaces. Find the smallest distance
from the surface to the origin in each case and determine the point(s) on the surface where
this minimal distance is achieved.

     x2 y 2 z 2
a)     +   +    = 1.
     4   9   16

b) −   +   +    = 1.
     4   9   16

        2   2     2
       x   y   z
c) −     −   +    = 1.
       4   9   16




                                         F c) Shortest distance of 4 at (0, 0, ±4) F


                                            2
        x2 y 2   z2
d) −     −    −    = 1.
       4   9    16




                                             F d) No quadric surface F
     2      2    2
e) 4x + 9y − z = 36.




                               F e) Shortest distance of 2 at(0, ±2, 0) F
f ) 5x2 + 7z 2 = 70.




                                                      √                √
                          F f) Shortest distance of       10 at(0, 0, ± 10) F



                                  3
 Consider the curve
                                  2x2 − 4xy + 5y 2 = 54.
The presence of the mixed term xy indicates that this is a standard object (ellipse or
hyperbola) which has been rotated to some degree so that its major and minor axes no
longer point in the x and y directions. To understand the curve we need to apply a specific
transformation that “unrotates” the curve into standard form.

Our first step is to rewrite the equation in terms of matrices.
                                                        T                  
           2            2                            x          2 −2        x
Claim: 2x − 4xy + 5y = 54 is equivalent to                                          = 54
                                                     y          −2 5        y
Proof:




Thus, we can express any quadratic curve in the form xT Ax where A is a symmetric
matrix by construction. As we saw in the last lecture, this means that the eigenvectors of
A will be orthogonal to one another. We can now calculate the complete set of eigenvalues
and eigenvectors of A.




                                             4
 So the eigenvalues of A are 1 and 6 with associated unit eigenvectors
                               2                        1 
                                √                       −√
                        v̂1 =  15  and v̂2 =  2 5 
                                                           
                                √                        √
                                  5                        5
These directions form the principal axes of the curve. If we define a new new orthogonal
coordinate system {X, Y } aligned with the vectors v̂1 and v̂2 , then the curve has the
simplest algebraic form. In this coordinate system the equation of the curve is

                                    1X 2 + 6Y 2 = 54.

Note the appearance of the eigenvalues! We can now identify the curve as an ellipse.
Rewriting the equation as            2  2
                                  X        Y
                                 √      +       =1
                                   54      3
we see that the closest points to the origin is ±3 units in the Y direction. In the {x, y}
coordinate system these points are
                                  3                        3 
                                −√                         +√
                      3v̂2 =       5          −3v̂2 =       5 
                                   6 ,                       6 .
                                                       
                                +√                         −√
                                    5                          5




                                            5
 Example 3 Express the equation of the surface

                        x2 + 2y 2 + 2z 2 + 4xy − 4xz + 6yz = 30

in terms of its principal axes X, Y and Z and hence determine the nature of the surface.
Find an orthogonal matrix P that transforms the surface to its principal axes,
                                                 
                                     x           X
                                    y =P Y 
                                      z          Z

Deduce the shortest distance from the surface to the origin and the {x, y, z} coordinates
of these closest point(s).




                                           6
                                      1     2
                                                     
                                    √    −√       0
                                     3      6          
                                  − √1 − √1         1 
                                 
                            F P =                  √   , −3X 2 + 3Y 2 + 5Z 2 = 30 F
                                      3     6        2 
                                  1      1          1 
                                    √     √         √
                                      3     6         2                          
                                                                   √         √0
F   Hyperboloid of one sheet with closest distance to the origin of 6 at ±  √3  F
                                                                                3

          You can now do Q 91 and 92
